2005.assessment:
Myanmar under military junta had opaque fiscal management. Large fiscal deficits
monetized through central bank printing. Public debt data unreliable. Natural gas
revenues from Yadana and Yetagun fields accrued to military-controlled entities
outside the regular budget. Fiscal system severely dysfunctional.

2006.assessment:
Fiscal deficits continued under military rule. High inflation partly driven by
fiscal monetization. Military spending consumed large share of budget. External
debt limited due to sanctions and limited borrowing access.

2007.assessment:
Fiscal pressures drove the subsidy removal that triggered the Saffron Revolution.
The junta's budget management remained opaque with off-budget military revenues.
Fiscal deficit significant but exact figures unreliable.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis added emergency fiscal pressures. The junta's refusal of
international aid complicated fiscal recovery. Natural gas revenues provided
some cushion. Fiscal management remained opaque and dysfunctional.

2009.assessment:
Fiscal management remained under junta control with opaque budget processes.
Natural gas revenues provided government income but not transparently managed.
Deficit monetization continued.

2010.assessment:
Fiscal management beginning to improve as election approached. Budget reform
discussions part of managed political transition planning. Natural gas revenues
key source of government income.

2011.assessment:
Fiscal reforms began with democratic transition. Budget process becoming more
transparent. Multiple exchange rate system began to be unified, improving
fiscal accounting. IMF engagement started.

2012.assessment:
Major fiscal reforms in 2012 with exchange rate unification. Budget management
improving substantially. External debt relief obtained from Paris Club creditors.
Fiscal position improving with growing revenues.

2013.assessment:
Fiscal position continued improving. Deficit manageable relative to GDP growth.
Government revenue rising with economic expansion and tax reforms. IMF technical
assistance supporting fiscal management improvements.

2014.assessment:
Fiscal health improving. Strong GDP growth boosting revenues. Public debt
declining as % of GDP. Government implementing tax and budget reforms.

2015.assessment:
Fiscal position broadly sound. Deficit manageable. NLD election victory
raised expectations for fiscal reform. Revenue improvements continuing
despite currency depreciation.

2016.assessment:
NLD government inherited broadly sound fiscal position. Continued reforms.
US sanctions removal improved investment climate and revenue potential.
Budget deficit manageable.

2017.assessment:
Fiscal health broadly stable. Rohingya crisis created some emergency
spending pressures in Rakhine but national fiscal position remained
manageable. IMF engagement continued.

2018.assessment:
Fiscal position broadly manageable. Deficit around 3-4% of GDP. Public
debt at moderate levels. Revenue collection improving with economic growth.

2019.assessment:
Fiscal position sound pre-coup. Deficit manageable. Myanmar pursuing fiscal
reforms with IMF support. Revenue mobilization improving. Public debt moderate.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 caused fiscal deterioration. Spending increased for health response
while revenues fell. Deficit widened but remained manageable with IMF
emergency financing assistance.

2021.assessment:
Coup caused severe fiscal deterioration. Government revenues collapsed as
civil disobedience movement paralyzed tax collection and public services.
Military spending surged. Junta began monetizing deficits again. IMF suspended
engagement. Fiscal situation moved toward unsustainability.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar Economic Monitor, December 2021

2022.assessment:
Fiscal situation unsustainable. Military government monetizing deficits through
central bank. Revenue collection collapsed in conflict zones. Western aid withdrawn.
Debt service difficulties. Economy in deep recession.

2023.assessment:
Fiscal collapse continued. Junta monetizing deficits, driving inflation.
Operation 1027 from October further disrupted territory under junta control,
reducing its revenue base. Fiscal data unreliable.

2024.assessment:
Junta fiscal position deteriorating as it controls less territory. Revenue base
shrinking. Military expenditures high. Monetization of deficits continues.
Complete fiscal collapse in many regions outside junta control.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under military junta rule had very low GDP per capita. The economy
was largely agricultural with significant state-run enterprises and limited
foreign investment. Official statistics unreliable under SPDC.

2006.assessment:
Myanmar economy showed nominal growth in 2006 but from a very low base.
Economy remains heavily controlled by the military junta. Significant
natural gas revenues captured by the state but not distributed broadly.

2007.assessment:
Nominal GDP per capita rose in 2007, partly reflecting currency effects.
The Saffron Revolution protests in September 2007 disrupted economic activity
and demonstrated deep public discontent with economic conditions under the junta.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis devastated the Irrawaddy Delta in May 2008, killing over 130,000
and causing massive economic damage. The sharp nominal GDP increase partly reflects
currency revaluation; real welfare did not improve commensurately.

2009.assessment:
Myanmar economy continued its nominal growth trajectory. However, living standards
remained very low for the majority of the population. Natural gas revenues and
Chinese investment were key drivers for the state, not broadly shared.

2010.assessment:
Myanmar's nominal GDP exceeded $1000 per capita in 2010. The managed elections
in November 2010 were widely condemned as fraudulent but began a nominal democratic
transition. Economic reforms were beginning to be discussed.

2011.assessment:
Myanmar began its political transition under President Thein Sein. Economic
reforms including exchange rate unification began, attracting more FDI. GDP
growth accelerated as international sanctions eased and investment came in.

2012.assessment:
Myanmar's GDP per capita remained broadly flat in USD terms as exchange rate
adjustments offset growth. Major economic reforms continued with exchange rate
unification. FDI inflows increased significantly as international isolation ended.

2013.assessment:
Myanmar continued economic reform trajectory. GDP growth strong in real terms
though USD value relatively flat. Tourism, manufacturing, and services expanded.
US and EU sanctions were progressively eased during this period.

2014.assessment:
Myanmar GDP per capita rose to approximately $1,293. Economic growth was robust
at around 8%. FDI inflows were high. The country was preparing for the 2015 NLD
elections. EITI candidacy obtained this year.

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita declined nominally in 2015 due to currency depreciation and
global commodity price falls. NLD won landslide election in November 2015,
beginning democratic civilian government. Growth continued in real kyat terms.

2016.assessment:
First year of NLD civilian government under Aung San Suu Kyi. GDP per capita
recovering from 2015 currency effects. Economy growing at ~7-8% in real terms.
Strong FDI continued. International business confidence high.

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita growth continued modestly. However, the Rohingya crisis escalated
dramatically in August 2017 with military crackdown, causing 700,000+ to flee to
Bangladesh and triggering international condemnation. Economic impact concentrated
in Rakhine State.

2018.assessment:
Myanmar GDP per capita continued moderate growth. International sanctions began
re-emerging following the Rohingya crisis and ICJ proceedings. FDI started to
decline from peak. Economy remained growing in real terms.

2019.assessment:
Myanmar reached its highest pre-coup GDP per capita in 2019. Economic growth
around 6.5% real terms. Despite international criticism over Rohingya situation,
business investment continued. NLD government continued economic reform program.

2020.assessment:
Myanmar GDP per capita peaked in 2020 despite COVID-19 pandemic. NLD won
second landslide election in November 2020 with over 80% of seats. The military
began contesting the results, setting the stage for the February 2021 coup.

2021.assessment:
The military coup of February 1, 2021 triggered a severe economic collapse.
GDP contracted by approximately 18%. Civil disobedience movement shut down
much of the economy. International sanctions were imposed. Banking system
stressed. Kyat depreciated sharply.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar Economic Monitor, December 2021

2022.assessment:
Myanmar economy continued to contract under military rule. Civil war escalated
with PDF resistance forces fighting the Tatmadaw across the country. Western
sanctions tightened. Currency severely depreciated. Humanitarian crisis deepened.
Data reliability very low as official statistics from junta are unreliable.

2023.assessment:
Myanmar remained in economic crisis under junta rule. Operation 1027 by ethnic
armed organizations and PDF forces from October 2023 captured significant territory,
further disrupting the economy. Real GDP growth negative in real terms. Data from
junta unreliable.

2024.assessment:
Myanmar continued under military junta with ongoing civil war. Economic statistics
from the junta are unreliable. Real living standards far below official figures.
Humanitarian crisis worsened. Resistance forces controlling large parts of
border areas and some urban areas by late 2024.

2005.assessment:
Inflation at 9.37% in 2005, elevated but relatively stable by Myanmar junta-era
standards. Price pressures driven by fiscal deficits and currency management.

2006.assessment:
Inflation surged to nearly 20% in 2006, driven by fuel price hikes and fiscal
pressures. This contributed to growing popular discontent that would peak with
the Saffron Revolution in 2007.

2007.assessment:
Inflation reached 35% in 2007. The junta's sudden removal of fuel subsidies
in August 2007 triggered the Saffron Revolution protests led by Buddhist monks.
Sharp price increases in fuel and basic commodities drove popular unrest.

2008.assessment:
Inflation remained very high at nearly 27% in 2008. Cyclone Nargis in May 2008
caused enormous damage and disrupted food production, adding further price
pressures. Global commodity price spikes also contributed.

2009.assessment:
Inflation collapsed to 1.47% in 2009 following the sharp spikes of 2007-2008.
Commodity price falls globally and the low base effect contributed. This may
also reflect data reliability issues with official statistics.

2010.assessment:
Inflation rebounded to 7.72% in 2010. The managed election year saw some
economic stabilization measures. Currency and monetary management remained
under military control.

2011.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 5% as the Thein Sein government began economic reforms.
Exchange rate unification and monetary policy improvements helped stabilize prices.
Start of democratic transition improved economic confidence.

2012.assessment:
Inflation very low in 2012, likely due to exchange rate reform effects and
data methodology changes. Myanmar unified its multiple exchange rates in 2012,
significantly changing price data comparisons.

2013.assessment:
Inflation at moderate 5.6% in 2013. Myanmar's economic opening continued with
rising investment and consumption. Price pressures moderate as supply-side
capacity expanded.

2014.assessment:
Inflation at 4.95% in 2014, broadly stable. Myanmar's economy was growing
strongly with rising FDI and improving macroeconomic management.

2015.assessment:
Inflation picked up to 9.45% in 2015 as kyat depreciated and commodity
prices rose. NLD election victory in November. Elevated inflation remained a concern.

2016.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 6.93% under new NLD government. Price pressures
eased somewhat as currency stabilized.

2017.assessment:
Inflation fell to 4.57% in 2017. Monetary policy improving. Despite Rohingya
crisis in Rakhine, national-level price stability was maintained.

2018.assessment:
Inflation increased to 6.87% in 2018. Currency depreciation pressure and
rising import costs contributed.

2019.assessment:
Inflation at 8.83% in 2019, the highest of the NLD era. Food and fuel price
pressures and kyat depreciation drove inflation upward.

2021.assessment:
Following the February 2021 coup, Myanmar experienced severe currency depreciation
and supply disruptions. Estimates suggest inflation surged to 12-20%. Official
statistics from the junta are unreliable. Civil disobedience movement and banking
system stress caused major supply disruptions.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar Economic Monitor, December 2021

2022.assessment:
Myanmar experienced severe inflation in 2022, estimated at 18-22% by independent
analysts. Kyat depreciation, import disruptions, and supply chain collapse drove
very high inflation. Civil war conditions made reliable data collection impossible.

sources.citation: UN OCHA Myanmar Humanitarian Situation Report 2022

2023.assessment:
Inflation remained very high in 2023. Continued kyat depreciation, import
restrictions, and military expenditure fueling money supply growth drove
sustained price increases. Reliable data unavailable.

2024.assessment:
Inflation continued at elevated levels in 2024 as civil war persisted. The
junta's monetary financing of deficits and currency weakness kept price pressures
high. Reliable statistics unavailable.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under military junta maintained heavily controlled trade with limited
openness. Natural gas exports to Thailand were a major revenue source. Trade
data was not reliably reported to international databases. World Bank data
unavailable for this period.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation reports on Myanmar

2006.assessment:
Myanmar trade remained restricted. US sanctions limiting American trade.
China and Thailand as major partners. Official data not available in WDI.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution led to expanded US and EU sanctions, reducing trade with
Western partners. China and ASEAN trade continued. Economy remains moderately
closed relative to regional peers.

2008.assessment:
Trade continued to be restricted by Western sanctions. China and regional
partners dominate. Cyclone Nargis disrupted some trade but natural gas exports
to Thailand continued.

2009.assessment:
Trade openness remained limited. Economy predominantly agricultural with
limited manufactured exports. Western sanctions constrained trade with
US and EU countries.

2010.assessment:
Trade openness began increasing as Myanmar's economy expanded. Natural gas,
agricultural products, and timber were main exports. Sanctions constrained
Western trade links.

2011.assessment:
Democratic transition began to attract foreign attention. Western sanctions
began to ease. Trade openness started growing as Myanmar opened to international
business. ASEAN membership supported trade.

2012.assessment:
US and EU began suspending sanctions, opening trade. FDI surge anticipated
more exports. Exchange rate unification improved data quality for trade
statistics. Trade openness growing.

2013.assessment:
Trade grew substantially as Myanmar opened to international investment.
Garment exports to EU resumed with GSP preferential access restored.
Trade openness increasing toward moderate levels.

2014.assessment:
Myanmar's trade expanded significantly. Garment sector exports to EU growing.
FDI-linked imports rising. Trade openness increasing as country integrated
into regional value chains.

2015.assessment:
Trade continued growing. Garment exports major contributor. NLD election
victory improved trade prospects. Myanmar increasingly integrated into
ASEAN and global trade networks.

2016.assessment:
Trade openness continued growing under NLD government. US fully lifted
sanctions in October 2016, opening American market. Trade volumes
expanding rapidly.

2017.assessment:
Trade openness at moderate levels. Rohingya crisis triggered new US and EU
targeted sanctions beginning to emerge. Garment exports continued to grow.

2018.assessment:
Trade maintained at moderate openness. International sanctions re-emerging
over Rohingya but trade not comprehensively blocked. Economic integration
into ASEAN and China continuing.

2019.assessment:
Trade at peak pre-coup openness. Myanmar integrated into ASEAN production
networks. China and Thailand dominant partners. Garment exports to EU
strong.

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 significantly reduced trade in 2020. Export orders collapsed.
Garment factories partially closed. However, Chinese trade continued.

2021.assessment:
Coup dramatically reduced trade openness. Western sanctions immediately
cut trade with US and EU. Civil disobedience movement paralyzed ports
and logistics. Garment exports collapsed as buyers boycotted Myanmar.
Trade severely contracted.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar Economic Monitor, December 2021

2022.assessment:
Trade further restricted under comprehensive Western sanctions. China
trade continued. Civil war disrupting internal logistics and border trade.
Garment sector severely reduced.

sources.citation: UN OCHA Myanmar Humanitarian Situation Report 2022

2023.assessment:
Trade severely restricted. Civil war and sanctions continued limiting
trade openness. Economy increasingly dependent on China for trade and finance.
Operation 1027 from October 2023 further disrupted border trade routes.

2024.assessment:
Trade remains restricted under sanctions and civil war. Economy largely
dependent on China trade. Western markets largely inaccessible.
Reliable statistics unavailable.

2005.assessment:
Official unemployment in Myanmar is extremely low by ILO estimates, but this
reflects widespread underemployment and subsistence agriculture rather than
genuine full employment. Most workers in informal sector not captured as unemployed.

2006.assessment:
Official unemployment remains near zero. This reflects the agrarian structure
of Myanmar's economy where most people work in subsistence agriculture and
are classified as employed regardless of actual welfare or income levels.

2007.assessment:
Despite the Saffron Revolution economic disruption, official unemployment remained
negligible. The informal and subsistence economy absorbed labor market shocks.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis caused massive job losses in the Irrawaddy Delta, but this was
not captured in formal unemployment statistics. True labor market distress
much higher than official figures suggest.

2009.assessment:
Official unemployment negligible. Myanmar's labor force largely in agriculture
and informal sectors. True underemployment and disguised unemployment likely
much higher.

2010.assessment:
Official unemployment remains very low. Election year, managed political transition.
Formal employment beginning to grow with economic opening.

2011.assessment:
Democratic transition began. Economic reforms starting. Official unemployment
very low; real jobs beginning to grow in manufacturing and services as foreign
investment attracted.

2012.assessment:
Formal employment growing as economic reforms attract FDI. Garment sector
expanded. Official unemployment remains negligible by ILO model estimates.

2013.assessment:
Myanmar's labor market benefiting from economic opening. Manufacturing jobs
growing. Official unemployment very low by ILO model estimates.

2014.assessment:
Official unemployment very low. Economic growth driving job creation across
manufacturing and services. Garment, construction, and telecom sectors expanding.

2015.assessment:
Official unemployment negligible. NLD election victory signaled continued
economic opening. Labor market improving with formal sector growth.

2016.assessment:
Official unemployment remains very low under civilian NLD government. Economic
growth continued. Formal sector employment expanding.

2017.assessment:
Unemployment still very low nationally. Rohingya crisis in Rakhine caused
local displacement and job losses, but this is not captured adequately in
national statistics.

2018.assessment:
Official unemployment very low. Economy growing despite international pressure
over Rohingya situation. Formal sector employment expanding.

2019.assessment:
Official unemployment extremely low in 2019 pre-coup. Myanmar's labor market
showed very low formal unemployment. Real jobs growing in manufacturing and
services sectors.

2020.assessment:
Unemployment rose in 2020 due to COVID-19 impacts. Garment factories closed
as export orders collapsed. This is the highest unemployment rate recorded before
the 2021 coup. Real unemployment much higher than official figures.

2021.assessment:
Unemployment spiked in the coup year. Civil disobedience movement shut down
much of the formal economy. Banking crisis, factory closures, and economic
contraction drove mass job losses. Real unemployment far higher than 4.34%.
The WB estimate significantly underestimates the crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar Economic Monitor, December 2021

2022.assessment:
Official unemployment estimates show decline from 2021, which is implausible
given ongoing civil war and economic contraction. These figures should be
treated with extreme caution. Real unemployment and underemployment much higher.

2023.assessment:
ILO modeled estimates show low official unemployment but this is not reliable
during civil war. Real labor market conditions severely deteriorated. Many
people displaced and unable to work.

2024.assessment:
Official estimates show very low unemployment, which is implausible given
ongoing civil war conditions. Real labor market severely disrupted by conflict,
displacement, and economic contraction.

2025.assessment:
ILO modeled estimate. Real labor market conditions continue to deteriorate
under ongoing civil war. Official figures meaningless for policy purposes.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar maintained ASEAN membership but faced significant Western isolation.
US and EU had limited formal relations beyond sanctions monitoring. China,
India, and ASEAN partners maintained normal relations. UN had Special Rapporteur
on Myanmar. Significant isolation from Western bloc while integrated with
Asian neighbors.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
Significant Western diplomatic isolation continued. Myanmar blocked from ASEAN
chairmanship in 2006 due to international pressure. ASEAN partners critical of
junta's handling of political situation. China and India maintained strong ties.

sources.citation: Reuters reports on Myanmar ASEAN chairmanship

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution crackdown deepened diplomatic isolation from West. US
and EU recalled ambassadors for consultations. UN Secretary-General sent
envoy. China blocked UN Security Council action. Myanmar approaching pariah
status with Western nations.

sources.citation: UN Security Council discussions on Myanmar 2007

2008.assessment:
Significant isolation continued. Cyclone Nargis relief effort created some
limited diplomatic engagement as junta was forced to accept some international
assistance. Normal ASEAN relations maintained. Western isolation continued.

2009.assessment:
Obama administration signaled openness to engagement while maintaining sanctions.
Some diplomatic dialogue resumed. Significant isolation continued with Western
nations but Asian engagement normal.

2010.assessment:
Diplomatic isolation continued with Western nations. ASEAN engagement normal.
Elections did not change Western diplomatic stance. ASSK released improved
diplomatic climate marginally.

2011.assessment:
Major diplomatic opening. Thein Sein's reforms attracted Western diplomatic
engagement. Secretary Clinton visited in December 2011. US and EU began easing
isolation. Myanmar moved rapidly from significant isolation toward partial integration.

sources.citation: US Secretary Clinton visit to Myanmar, December 2011

2012.assessment:
Rapid diplomatic normalization. US restored full diplomatic relations, nominated
ambassador. EU, Australia, Japan all expanded diplomatic engagement. Myanmar
integrated into international community rapidly. President Obama visited November 2012.

2013.assessment:
Myanmar broadly diplomatically integrated. Full Western diplomatic relations
restored. Myanmar chairmanship of ASEAN (2014). Active participation in
international forums. Some residual concerns about Rohingya reducing full
integration.

2014.assessment:
Myanmar chaired ASEAN. Full diplomatic relations with all major powers.
Obama visited again. Active participant in international forums. Rohingya
situation causing some friction but not isolation.

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration. NLD election victory celebrated internationally.
Myanmar widely praised for successful democratic transition. Active member of
ASEAN, UN, and international forums. Full bilateral relations with all major powers.

2016.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration continued under NLD. Aung San Suu Kyi as State
Counsellor active internationally. ARSA attacks in Rakhine began to erode some
diplomatic goodwill but overall integration remained full.

2017.assessment:
Rohingya crisis caused major diplomatic deterioration. ASSK faced international
condemnation but remained in power. US, EU, and international organizations
strongly critical. Myanmar partially isolated over Rohingya but maintained ASEAN
relations and China support. ICJ proceedings began.

2018.assessment:
Continued partial isolation. Myanmar government (including ASSK) condemned by
UN Fact-Finding Mission. Some Western ambassadors reduced contact. ASSK's
honorary citizenship revoked by several countries. China and ASEAN maintained
normal relations.

2019.assessment:
Partial diplomatic isolation continuing. ICJ genocide proceedings began
(The Gambia v Myanmar). ASSK defended Myanmar at ICJ. Western relationships
strained. China and Russia support at UN. ASEAN maintained normal relations.

sources.citation: ICJ The Gambia v Myanmar proceedings

2020.assessment:
Partial isolation continuing over Rohingya. Western relations strained but not
broken. NLD election attracted international attention. Pre-coup diplomatic
situation strained but functional.

2021.assessment:
Coup triggered massive diplomatic isolation from West. US, UK, EU, Canada
condemned coup and refused to recognize SAC junta. Ambassadors withdrawn or
reduced to lower level. Western governments suspended assistance. ASEAN
imposed its own "Five-Point Consensus" which junta largely ignored. China
and Russia blocked UN action. Myanmar approaching pariah status.

sources.citation: UN General Assembly Resolution on Myanmar 2021

2022.assessment:
Western diplomatic isolation near-complete for junta. Junta not recognized by
US, EU, UK and most Western nations. ASEAN suspended Myanmar from political
meetings. NUG recognized by some international actors. China and Russia maintain
relations with junta.

sources.citation: ASEAN suspension of Myanmar political participation

2023.assessment:
Continuing diplomatic isolation for junta from Western bloc. China maintains
relations. ASEAN diplomatic engagement suspended. NUG gaining more recognition
from some Western parliaments and cities. Junta's international legitimacy eroding.

2024.assessment:
Junta remains diplomatically isolated from Western world. As resistance forces
capture more territory, junta's international standing continues to erode.
China still engaging. ASEAN continuing suspension of junta's political participation.

2025.assessment:
Continuing diplomatic isolation. Junta's pariah status with Western nations
maintained. Civil war ongoing. No prospect of diplomatic normalization.

2005.assessment:
FDI at nearly 2% of GDP in 2005. Despite Western sanctions, China and ASEAN
neighbors (especially Singapore and Thailand) invested in Myanmar's gas, mining,
and infrastructure. Western investment blocked by sanctions.

2006.assessment:
FDI broadly stable at moderate levels despite Western sanctions. Regional
investors from China, Thailand, Singapore dominant. Natural gas and extractive
sector attracted most investment.

2007.assessment:
FDI increased in 2007 despite Saffron Revolution. Chinese investment in
Myitsone dam and other projects drove inflows. Saffron Revolution did not
significantly deter regional investors.

2008.assessment:
FDI declined slightly in 2008. Cyclone Nargis reduced some investor confidence
but Chinese investment continued in energy and infrastructure.

2009.assessment:
FDI at 2.92% of GDP in 2009. Chinese and regional investment continued at
moderate levels. Energy sector (natural gas) and Myitsone dam dominant.

2010.assessment:
FDI at 1.82% of GDP. Election year with uncertainty reduced some investment.
Chinese and regional investment continuing in energy and infrastructure.

2011.assessment:
FDI increased significantly as democratic transition attracted new investor
interest. Western investors beginning to consider Myanmar. Chinese and regional
investment continued. Democratic transition premium attracting investor optimism.

2012.assessment:
FDI moderated from 2011 peak but remained solid. Western sanctions easing
attracted more investors. Exchange rate reform improved investment environment.
Multiple Western and Asian companies entering Myanmar market.

2013.assessment:
FDI growing as Myanmar opened further. Western companies entering. Telecom
sector opened to foreign investment in 2013-14 with Telenor and Ooredoo winning
licenses. Hotel, banking, and retail FDI expanding.

2014.assessment:
FDI at 3.32% of GDP. Myanmar attracting strong foreign investment ahead of
2015 elections. Manufacturing, telecom, tourism, and services sectors growing.

2015.assessment:
FDI surged to 6.85% of GDP in 2015, one of highest rates globally. Election
year excitement and normalization of relations drove massive inflows. Special
Economic Zones, manufacturing, and services attracted investment across sectors.

2016.assessment:
FDI remained high under NLD government. Strong investment in manufacturing,
energy, and real estate. NLD government welcoming to foreign investment.
ARSA attacks created some late-year uncertainty.

2017.assessment:
FDI peaked at 7.27% of GDP in 2017 despite Rohingya crisis. Large Chinese
infrastructure investments (BRI-related) and Kyaukphyu deep-sea port project
drove inflows. Many Western companies stayed despite Rohingya concerns.

2018.assessment:
FDI declined sharply to 2.61% as Rohingya crisis impact hit investor confidence.
Some Western companies divested or withdrew. Chinese investment continued but
smaller scale. Currency depreciation also affected USD denominated figures.

2019.assessment:
FDI stabilized at 2.31% of GDP. Myanmar's investment environment remained
challenging due to Rohingya concerns but continued. Manufacturing and energy
sectors maintained investment flows.

2020.assessment:
FDI at 2.41% of GDP despite COVID-19. Myanmar received some FDI in manufacturing
as part of China+1 strategy. NLD re-election signaled political stability
(pre-coup). FDI maintained.

2021.assessment:
Official FDI shows 3.11% but this is likely distorted by pre-coup commitments
and post-coup Chinese infrastructure investments. Real new FDI collapsed as
Western investors fled after coup. Western garment brands withdrew. Net new
investment minimal; figures reflect prior commitments.

2022.assessment:
FDI declined under sanctions and civil war. Western investors largely absent.
Chinese FDI in energy and infrastructure continued. Civil war creating investment
risk. Official figures of uncertain reliability.

2023.assessment:
Official FDI data of low reliability during civil war. Chinese investments in
oil pipeline, port, and other infrastructure continued. Operation 1027 disrupted
northern areas. Real new investment minimal outside Chinese BRI-related projects.

2024.assessment:
FDI declined to 1.48% officially. Civil war and sanctions continuing to
deter most investors. Chinese strategic investments continued. Very low
real new private investment. Data highly uncertain.

2005.assessment:
No foreign military forces stationed in Myanmar. The SPDC military junta
maintained full sovereignty over Myanmar's territory. China provided military
equipment and technical advisors but no combat forces. No UN peacekeeping mission.

2006.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Myanmar maintained full sovereignty. China continued
military equipment supply. No peacekeeping mission. Junta maintained control
of armed forces without foreign military presence.

2007.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Myanmar. Full military sovereignty maintained
by junta. China and India maintained arms sales relationships. No UN peacekeeping.

2008.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Myanmar rejected most foreign assistance including
military support after Cyclone Nargis. Full sovereignty maintained.

2009.assessment:
No foreign military forces in Myanmar. Full sovereignty maintained. China
arms supply continued. Military relations with Russia also maintained.

2010.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Full sovereignty maintained by junta. Military
technology from China, Russia. No foreign combat or peacekeeping forces.

2011.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Democratic transition began but military maintained
full sovereign control over armed forces. US military advisors began limited
engagement but no combat or peacekeeping forces.

2012.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Myanmar opened to US military engagement including
officer training programs and limited advisors as part of normalization.
Sovereignty fully maintained.

2013.assessment:
Limited US military training engagement. No foreign combat forces. Myanmar
maintaining sovereign military authority. International military training
cooperation expanding.

2014.assessment:
Limited US and international military training and engagement. Myanmar military
increasingly engaging internationally. No foreign combat forces. Full sovereignty.

2015.assessment:
Limited international military training continuing. NLD election victory
did not change military sovereignty arrangements. No foreign combat forces.

2016.assessment:
NLD government in power. Military maintained autonomous command. No foreign
combat forces. Limited international military engagement continued.

2017.assessment:
US suspended military training and cooperation following Rohingya crackdown.
No foreign forces present. Myanmar military conducting operations independently.
Full sovereign control of military operations.

sources.citation: US suspension of military engagement with Myanmar 2017

2018.assessment:
No significant foreign military presence. US military ties largely suspended.
China maintained military equipment supply. Full sovereignty. Myanmar military
operating without significant foreign military presence.

2019.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Full Myanmar military sovereignty. US military
ties largely suspended. China arms supply continued. Russia also selling arms.

2020.assessment:
No foreign military forces. Pre-coup period. Myanmar military operating
independently. China and Russia as main arms suppliers.

2021.assessment:
No formal foreign military forces. Junta received Chinese and Russian arms.
No UN peacekeeping deployed (China and Russia blocked). Chinese military
advisors reportedly providing some support. Myanmar's military sovereignty
maintained in its controlled areas.

2022.assessment:
No formal foreign military forces. Russia and China supplying arms and jets to
junta. Jet fuel for air force sourced from Russia. No UN peacekeeping. Junta
dependent on Chinese and Russian arms while maintaining formal sovereignty.

sources.citation: SIPRI Arms Transfers 2022; Human Rights Watch on arms to Myanmar

2023.assessment:
No formal foreign military forces. Junta dependent on Chinese and Russian arms.
China concerned about instability at its border following Operation 1027 affecting
northern Shan State. China allegedly facilitating some ceasefire negotiations.

2024.assessment:
No formal foreign combat forces. China and Russia continue to arm the junta.
China balancing support for junta vs protecting its border interests. No UN
peacekeeping deployment.

2025.assessment:
Continuing Chinese and Russian arms to junta. No UN peacekeeping. Civil war
ongoing.

2005.assessment:
Ongoing displacement from ethnic conflicts. Karen/Kayin refugees in Thailand
had been accumulating since 1984. Estimated 150,000+ Karen refugees in
Thailand. Slow but steady displacement from ethnic conflicts. No mass exodus
events in this year.

2006.assessment:
Continued displacement from ethnic conflicts. Karen camps in Thailand continued
to grow. Some displacement from Shan State conflict. No major new displacement
events nationally in this specific year.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution caused some displacement as political activists fled to
Thailand. But no mass civilian displacement event. Ongoing ethnic conflict
displacement continued at low levels.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis internally displaced 750,000+ people but most were not refugees
(cross-border). Ongoing ethnic conflict continued to produce small outflows.
Karen camps in Thailand continued.

2009.assessment:
Kokang conflict displaced 37,000+ into China from Shan State. Karen border
displacement continued in Thailand. Ongoing low-level cross-border flows.

2010.assessment:
Low-level displacement from ongoing ethnic conflicts. Karen, Shan, and other
ethnic minorities in border areas continued to produce small refugee flows.

2011.assessment:
Kachin conflict resumed in June 2011 created new IDPs and some cross-border
displacement into China. Karen displacement in Thailand continued. Moderate
outflows from new Kachin conflict.

2012.assessment:
Rakhine sectarian violence in June and October 2012 displaced 140,000+ Rohingya
and Rakhine internally. Rohingya sea crossings to Thailand and Malaysia increased.
Kachin conflict continued to produce IDPs. Significant displacement escalation.

2013.assessment:
Rohingya boat people crisis escalating. Thousands crossing Andaman Sea to
Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia. Violence against Rohingya continuing. Kachin
displacement continued. Anti-Muslim violence in central Myanmar added displacement.

2014.assessment:
Rohingya boat people crisis continued. Thailand crackdowns on trafficking
networks in 2015 revealed mass graves. Ongoing Kachin and Shan conflict
displacement. Moderate outflows from multiple sources.

2015.assessment:
Andaman Sea crisis in May 2015 with thousands of Rohingya abandoned on boats.
Continued displacement from ethnic conflicts. NCA signing reduced some
conflict displacement. Rohingya crisis at significant level.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2015; Andaman Sea crisis

2016.assessment:
ARSA attacks in October 2016 and military response displaced additional
Rohingya to Bangladesh. Ethnic conflict displacement continuing. Displacement
at moderate level.

2017.assessment:
Catastrophic mass displacement. August 2017 military crackdown drove 700,000+
Rohingya to Bangladesh within weeks. This was one of the fastest mass
displacement events in modern history. Myanmar's population was ~54 million,
so 700,000 represents ~1.3% of population. One of the worst displacement
crises globally in 2017.

2018.assessment:
Post-Rohingya crisis; slow continued outflows. Repatriation not occurring
due to unsafe conditions. Arakan Army conflict in Rakhine continuing.
Kachin, Shan conflict displacement continuing. Stock of refugees high but
new flows reduced from 2017 peak.

2019.assessment:
AA-Tatmadaw conflict in Rakhine created new IDPs and some Rakhine Buddhist
displacement. Rohingya remaining in Myanmar still displaced within Rakhine.
Kachin/Shan conflict displacement continuing.

2020.assessment:
Continued moderate displacement from AA conflict (ceasefire Nov 2020),
Kachin, and Shan conflicts. Rohingya repatriation not occurring. Total
refugee and IDP stock high but new flows moderate.

2021.assessment:
Coup triggered massive new displacement. Over 300,000 newly displaced within
Myanmar by end of 2021. Cross-border flows to Thailand and India increased
significantly. UNHCR documented major IDP crisis. Coup-related displacement
significant additional outflows.

2022.assessment:
Displacement continued at high rate throughout civil war. Over 1 million
IDPs in Myanmar by end of 2022. Cross-border flows to Thailand, India,
Bangladesh. Total displaced (internal + external) reaching catastrophic levels.

2023.assessment:
Operation 1027 from October 2023 triggered major new displacement as fighting
swept through northern Shan State. Total IDPs exceeding 2 million. Among the
world's largest internal displacement crises. Cross-border flows continuing.

2024.assessment:
Displacement continuing at catastrophic levels in 2024. Total IDPs exceeding
3 million by mid-2024 according to OCHA. Largest IDP crisis in Asia. Cross-border
flows to Thailand, India, Bangladesh. Civilian casualties from air strikes
adding to displacement.

2025.assessment:
Displacement continuing at very high levels. Civil war ongoing. No prospect
of significant returns while fighting continues.

2005.assessment:
US comprehensive sanctions under Burmese Freedom and Democracy Act (2003) and
subsequent executive orders blocked imports from Myanmar and froze assets of
junta leaders and associates. EU maintained arms embargo and targeted sanctions
on regime officials. These sanctions had significant impact on garment industry
and financial access.

sources.citation: EU Council Common Position on Myanmar

2006.assessment:
Sanctions regime continued with US and EU sanctions. US blocked all imports
from Myanmar. EU arms embargo and targeted sanctions. Japan limited aid.
Australia had targeted sanctions. These were among Asia's most sanctioned regimes.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution triggered tightening of sanctions. US expanded sanctions
via EO 13448 targeting regime leaders, their families, and business associates.
Sanctions expanded post-crackdown. EU strengthened measures.

2008.assessment:
Sanctions remained comprehensive. Cyclone Nargis emergency led to temporary
humanitarian exceptions and some emergency aid flows. Core sanctions regime
unchanged. Broad economic sanctions targeting junta and connected businesses.

2009.assessment:
Obama administration initially maintained sanctions but signaled willingness
to engage. US began limited diplomatic engagement while maintaining sanctions.
Sanctions regime broadly unchanged.

2010.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions maintained despite managed elections. US did not
recognize election results. Aung San Suu Kyi's release in November did not
immediately change sanctions status. Broad economic sanctions continued.

2011.assessment:
As democratic transition began, US and EU began slowly easing sanctions.
Secretary Clinton visited Myanmar in December 2011. Some targeted sanctions
relaxed. But core comprehensive sanctions remained. Sanctions were suspended
rather than removed as process began.

2012.assessment:
Major sanctions easing in 2012. EU suspended (not lifted) most sanctions
after NLD by-election. US eased many restrictions including allowing investment
with reporting requirements. Import ban lifted for most goods. Targeted sanctions
on military individuals maintained.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Myanmar sanctions easing 2012

2013.assessment:
Continued easing. US lifted most economic sanctions in July 2012, with reporting
requirements remaining for investment. EU formally lifted most sanctions in 2013
retaining only arms embargo. Targeted sanctions on specific military officials
and gems trade maintained.

2014.assessment:
Residual targeted sanctions on military officials and arms embargo maintained.
Otherwise open to investment and trade. Myanmar hosting ASEAN summit. Further
normalization of international relations.

2015.assessment:
Residual targeted sanctions maintained on military individuals. US continued
to normalize relations as NLD won elections. EU arms embargo maintained. No
comprehensive economic sanctions.

2016.assessment:
US fully lifted national emergency and most remaining sanctions on Myanmar
in October 2016 following NLD government's first year in power. Only targeted
sanctions on specific military individuals remained. Essentially no sanctions.

sources.citation: US Executive Order 13742, October 2016 - Terminating Myanmar sanctions emergency

2017.assessment:
Rohingya crisis triggered new targeted sanctions. US imposed targeted sanctions
on military units and commanders involved in Rakhine operations. EU also began
new targeted measures. Not comprehensive sanctions but growing list of targeted
designations.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Myanmar new designations 2017

2018.assessment:
Targeted sanctions expanded. US sanctioned Commander-in-Chief Min Aung Hlaing
and other senior commanders. Growing list of military officials sanctioned.
EU expanded targeted measures. Sectoral impact limited but growing political
pressure.

2019.assessment:
Targeted sanctions continuing to expand on military officials. ICJ proceedings
began (The Gambia v Myanmar). Growing international legal and sanctions pressure
but not comprehensive economic sanctions at this stage.

2020.assessment:
Targeted sanctions continuing. Pre-coup, sanctions were growing but not comprehensive.
NLD re-elected November 2020. Sanctions mainly targeted military officials and
military-owned enterprises.

2021.assessment:
Coup triggered massive sanctions expansion. US EO 14014 re-established Myanmar
national emergency and expanded sanctions to junta leaders, military-owned
businesses (MEHL, MEC), and government entities. UK, EU, Canada also imposed
broad sanctions including on military conglomerate businesses. Sanctions moved
toward sectoral/broad economic level.

sources.citation: EU Council Regulation on Myanmar sanctions 2021

2022.assessment:
Comprehensive sanctions regime expanding. US, UK, EU, Canada targeted military
leaders, junta officials, military-owned businesses, state-owned enterprises.
Myanmar Oil and Gas Enterprise (MOGE) targeted. Approaching comprehensive status
but China and ASEAN not imposing sanctions.

2023.assessment:
Comprehensive Western sanctions regime in place. US, UK, EU, Canada, Australia
all have broad sanctions on junta officials, military businesses, and state
enterprises. But China and ASEAN countries not participating. Junta funding
largely from China and regional trade.

2024.assessment:
Comprehensive Western sanctions maintained and expanded. US imposed additional
sanctions on entities providing fuel and arms to the junta. EU maintained broad
sanctions. But China's support to junta limits impact of Western measures.

2025.assessment:
Comprehensive Western sanctions continue. Junta relying on China and regional
trade to circumvent Western sanctions. No indication of sanctions lifting while
civil war continues.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under military junta had essentially no civil liberties. Freedom House
rated it 7 (worst possible) on civil liberties. All media state-controlled or
censored. No freedom of assembly. Political prisoners including Aung San Suu Kyi.
Religious minorities persecuted. Pervasive surveillance.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005 - Burma/Myanmar

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at lowest level globally under SPDC. No media freedom,
assembly, or political activity outside junta-approved parameters. Forced labor
by military continued. Over 1,100 political prisoners held.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006 - Burma/Myanmar

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution crackdown demonstrated extreme suppression of civil liberties.
Protesters shot and beaten. Monasteries raided. Monks arrested. Internet shut
down. No freedom of assembly or expression. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2007 - Burma/Myanmar

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties at lowest level. Cyclone Nargis relief workers restricted,
showing continued junta control over civil society. Constitution approved
in sham referendum. Freedom House rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2008 - Burma/Myanmar

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties at minimum. ASSK re-arrested and tried. Political prisoners
increased. No media freedom. Assembly prohibited. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2009 - Burma/Myanmar

2010.assessment:
Civil liberties at minimum level. Managed elections in November with no genuine
freedoms. NLD boycott. Aung San Suu Kyi released from house arrest in November
after elections. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010 - Burma/Myanmar

2011.assessment:
Significant improvement in 2011 as democratic transition began. Press censorship
reduced. Political prisoners released in waves. Assembly rights somewhat expanded.
FH rated civil liberties at 6 (still very poor but improved from 7).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011 - Burma/Myanmar

2012.assessment:
Continued rapid improvement. Press censorship formally reduced. NLD registered
as political party. Aung San Suu Kyi elected to parliament. FH upgraded to
civil liberties 5. Major improvements in expression and assembly rights.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012 - Burma/Myanmar

2013.assessment:
Civil liberties improving. Press censorship formally ended. Private newspapers
re-emerged. But ethnic minorities still restricted. Anti-Muslim violence demonstrated
limits on religious liberty. FH rated civil liberties 5.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013 - Burma/Myanmar

2014.assessment:
Civil liberties at 5 per Freedom House. Significant freedoms compared to
junta era but major restrictions remained. Muslims in Rakhine essentially
stateless with no civil rights. Ethnic minorities in conflict zones severely
restricted. National press expanding.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014 - Burma/Myanmar

2015.assessment:
Civil liberties improving with elections. Press relatively freer. Assembly
rights expanded. NLD campaigned openly. Rohingya excluded from elections and
citizenship. Significant restrictions remained particularly for minorities.
FH rated 5.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015 - Burma/Myanmar

2016.assessment:
NLD government improved civil liberties in civilian domains. Press relatively
freer. But military retained control over Rakhine operations. ARSA attacks in
October triggered military crackdown, restricting rights in Rakhine. FH rated 5.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016 - Burma/Myanmar

2017.assessment:
Significant regression in 2017 due to Rohingya crisis. Military crackdown
in Rakhine systematically violated rights of Rohingya through mass killing,
rape, and arson. Freedom of information about Rakhine restricted. Reporters
prevented from accessing affected areas. FH downgraded civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017 - Burma/Myanmar

2018.assessment:
Civil liberties continued declining. Two Reuters journalists jailed for
reporting on Rohingya killings under state secrets law. Media intimidation
increased. Rakhine emergency restrictions continued. FH rated 6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018 - Burma/Myanmar

2019.assessment:
Civil liberties continued at restricted level. Press freedom restricted with
journalists jailed under telecommunications and state secrets laws. Rohingya
in Bangladesh with no rights. Internet shutdowns in Rakhine and Chin states.
FH rated 6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019 - Burma/Myanmar

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties restricted. Internet shutdowns continued in Rakhine and Chin
for over a year (longest shutdown globally at the time). NLD election conducted
but Rohingya unable to vote. Press freedom restricted. FH rated 6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020 - Burma/Myanmar

2021.assessment:
Coup caused catastrophic reversal to no civil liberties. Internet shut down.
Protesters shot in streets. Political parties disbanded. Thousands arrested.
Martial law declared in many areas. All freedoms of expression, assembly,
and movement severely curtailed. FH returned to 7 (worst).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021 - Burma/Myanmar

2022.assessment:
No civil liberties under junta. Widespread arbitrary detention, torture, and
extrajudicial killing documented. Media entirely under junta control. Internet
restricted. Martial law broad. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2022 - Burma/Myanmar

2023.assessment:
Civil liberties at minimum. Air strikes on civilian areas. Mass arbitrary
detention. Villages burned. Forced military recruitment. No freedom of press,
assembly, or movement. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2023 - Burma/Myanmar

2024.assessment:
No civil liberties in junta-controlled areas. Ongoing violations including
air strikes on civilian gatherings, arbitrary detention, torture. In resistance-held
areas, local administrations provide some civil space. FH rated 7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2024 - Burma/Myanmar

2025.assessment:
Civil liberties remain at minimum under ongoing civil war and junta rule.
Systematic repression in junta-controlled areas. Some civil space in resistance
areas. No national improvement expected in near term.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2025

2005.assessment:
Military junta under Than Shwe appeared unified. SPDC presented a unified
front with little visible dissension. Senior General Than Shwe consolidated
power over rivals including Khin Nyunt (purged in 2004). Junta cohesion
high but strictly enforced from above.

sources.citation: ICG Asia Report: Myanmar - Prospects for Change

2006.assessment:
Military junta remained cohesive under Than Shwe's authority. Capital moved to
Naypyidaw from Yangon in 2006, reflecting leadership's security concerns.
Elite unity maintained through patronage and fear.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution tested junta cohesion. Military leadership maintained unity
in ordering crackdown. No significant defections visible. Senior leadership
consolidated by Than Shwe who ordered suppression.

2008.assessment:
Junta remained cohesive. Cyclone Nargis response showed unified decision-making
at the top. Constitution approved in stage-managed referendum. Leadership
preparing managed transition while retaining control.

2009.assessment:
Junta cohesion maintained. Election preparations underway with managed transition
planning. No significant visible elite splits. Than Shwe remained dominant figure.

2010.assessment:
Managed transition underway. Some elite differentiation between military's
political wing (USDP) and Thein Sein's reformist tendency. Nominal cohesion
maintained through managed succession designed by Than Shwe.

2011.assessment:
Thein Sein emerged as genuine reformer, potentially diverging from harder-line
military elements. Significant competition between reform-oriented vs conservative
military factions. Civilian-military tension beginning to emerge. Managed within system.

sources.citation: ICG Myanmar: A New Peace Initiative, 2011

2012.assessment:
Significant competition between reformists and conservatives within military
system. Thein Sein pursuing rapid reform pace. NLD's parliamentary entry added
new political dynamic. Tensions managed within system.

2013.assessment:
Elite competition continued between reform-oriented and conservative military
factions. Anti-Muslim violence from nationalist monks suggests some elite
tolerance of extremism as political tool. Managed competition within system.

2014.assessment:
Elite competition intensifying as 2015 elections approached. Military vs NLD
rivalry dominant political dynamic. Min Aung Hlaing emerged as powerful figure
within military. Managed competition between civilian and military elites.

2015.assessment:
NLD landslide election created major elite transition. Military-backed USDP
lost badly. Military leadership (Min Aung Hlaing) began asserting autonomy.
ASSK negotiating power-sharing arrangements. Significant open competition.

2016.assessment:
Civilian-military dual power arrangement. NLD government and military operating
as parallel power structures. Open tension over military's autonomy in Rakhine
operations. Elite divided between NLD civilian leadership and military command.

2017.assessment:
Civilian-military rupture over Rohingya. ASSK refused to criticize military
operations. Open factional conflict between civilian and military elite directions.
ASSK's international reputation collapsed. Internal contradictions in the governing arrangement.

2018.assessment:
Civilian-military tension continued. NLD and military operating as distinct
power centers. Factional conflict between civilian leadership and Min Aung
Hlaing's military remained open.

2019.assessment:
Continued civilian-military dual power with factional tensions. Military
expanded operations against Arakan Army without civilian authorization.
Military elite increasingly assertive of independent political role.

2020.assessment:
NLD landslide election exacerbated civil-military tensions. Military disputed
results. Min Aung Hlaing began open power struggle with NLD government.
Active power struggle in final months precipitating the February 2021 coup.

2021.assessment:
Military seized power February 1. SAC junta in full control of military.
NUG/CRPH formed in opposition. Complete fragmentation between junta and
resistance. Within resistance, NLD/NUG, PDFs, and EAOs have different
objectives creating complex multi-faction dynamics.

2022.assessment:
Complete elite fragmentation. Junta, NUG, multiple EAOs, and PDFs each
pursuing different strategies. Even within resistance, coordination imperfect.
National elite fragmentation at maximum.

2023.assessment:
Elite fragmentation continued with multiple armed actors. Operation 1027
showed Three Brotherhood Alliance coordination but separate from NUG/PDF.
Junta internal cohesion uncertain as it loses territory.

2024.assessment:
Elite fragmentation complete. Junta losing territory and potentially internal
cohesion. Multiple resistance factions with different agendas. No unified
national elite. Ongoing civil war with no resolution.

2025.assessment:
Elite fragmentation persisting. Civil war ongoing. Multiple competing actors
with no unified governance framework.

2005.assessment:
Under SPDC military junta, Myanmar had no functioning democratic institutions.
The State Peace and Development Council ruled by decree. No legislature, no
independent courts, and no free elections. All state institutions subordinated
to military command. Rated 7 (worst) by Freedom House for political rights.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005 - Burma/Myanmar

2006.assessment:
No functioning democratic institutions under SPDC. Military council ruled
by decree. Judiciary subservient to military command. National Convention
for constitution-drafting was stage-managed with excluded opposition parties.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006 - Burma/Myanmar

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution demonstrated total absence of legitimate institutions.
Military suppressed protests. No parliament, no independent courts. The junta's
constitutional convention continued as a facade. Rated among worst in world.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2007 - Burma/Myanmar

2008.assessment:
New constitution approved in referendum timed during Cyclone Nargis (widely
condemned). Constitution reserved 25% of parliament seats for military and
barred Aung San Suu Kyi from becoming president. Institutions still under
complete military control.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2008 - Burma/Myanmar

2009.assessment:
Pre-election preparations under military control. No genuine legislative,
judicial, or executive institutions operating independently. All institutions
subordinated to SPDC. Aung San Suu Kyi re-detained following trial.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2009 - Burma/Myanmar

2010.assessment:
Managed elections held in November 2010. Widely condemned as fraudulent.
NLD boycotted elections. Military-backed USDP won overwhelming majority.
Institutions remained entirely subordinated to military command. No genuine
electoral competition or independent courts.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010 - Burma/Myanmar

2011.assessment:
Democratic transition government of Thein Sein took power in March 2011.
Parliament began meeting. Some political reforms started including releasing
prisoners and media reforms. Institutions still heavily military-dominated
but beginning to function. Partial improvement from pure junta rule.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011 - Burma/Myanmar

2012.assessment:
By-elections in April 2012 allowed NLD to participate; Aung San Suu Kyi won
a seat. Parliament gaining more genuine function. Press censorship reduced.
Courts still not independent. Military retained major political role under
constitution. Significant improvement from previous years.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012 - Burma/Myanmar

2013.assessment:
Continued institutional improvements. Media censorship formally ended.
Parliament active but military retaining 25% quota in legislature.
Courts improving but not independent. Multi-party political competition
emerging but constraints remained.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013 - Burma/Myanmar

2014.assessment:
Continued gradual reform. Parliament functioning with genuine debate.
Civil society expanding. Elections scheduled for 2015. Military retained
25% quota and veto on constitutional reform. Rated 'Partly Free' by FH.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014 - Burma/Myanmar

2015.assessment:
NLD won November 2015 elections in a landslide in what were widely considered
credible elections. Parliament functioning. Major institutional milestone for
Myanmar. Military retained constitution-mandated 25% seats and service chiefs
appointments.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015 - Burma/Myanmar

2016.assessment:
NLD took power April 2016. Civilian-led government with dual-power arrangement
with military. Aung San Suu Kyi in State Counsellor role (created due to
constitutional bar on presidency). Parliament functioning. Courts still
not independent. Military held key ministries (Home, Defense, Border Affairs).

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016 - Burma/Myanmar

2017.assessment:
Civilian government continued under NLD but military retained autonomy over
Rakhine operations. Institutional functioning partially improved in civilian
domains. Military's independent operations in Rakhine outside civilian oversight
demonstrated limits of civilian control.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017 - Burma/Myanmar

2018.assessment:
Civilian government functioning in its domain but military retained autonomy.
Courts not independent. Two Reuters journalists arrested for reporting on
Rohingya killings, demonstrating limits on press freedom. Institutional
functioning partially improved from pre-2011 era but far from full democracy.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018 - Burma/Myanmar

2019.assessment:
Institutional functioning broadly stable. Parliament active. Civilian government
operating within its constrained mandate. Military continued autonomous control
of security forces. ICJ proceedings on Rohingya genocide.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019 - Burma/Myanmar

2020.assessment:
NLD won second election in November 2020. Military began contesting results.
Civilian institutions functioning in electoral process. COVID-19 disrupted some
functions. Military's refusal to accept results set stage for coup.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020 - Burma/Myanmar

2021.assessment:
Military coup February 1, 2021 dissolved all civilian institutions. NLD
government members arrested. Parliament dissolved. Courts under military
control. SAC (State Administration Council) military junta replaced all
institutions. Complete institutional collapse for democratic governance.
NUG/CRPH established as parallel government in exile.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021 - Burma/Myanmar

2022.assessment:
Junta rule with no legitimate institutions. Military tribunals operating.
NUG as parallel government lacking territorial control. Courts used as
instruments of repression. Civil war preventing any institutional function.
Among worst rated globally by Freedom House.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2022 - Burma/Myanmar

2023.assessment:
Junta institutions collapsed or nominal in much of country. Operation 1027
further undermined junta's institutional reach. NUG/CRPH parallel government
and EAO administrations filling gaps in territories outside junta control.
Multiple competing institutional frameworks.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2023 - Burma/Myanmar

2024.assessment:
Civil war continuing with junta controlling less territory. No legitimate
institutions functional nationwide. Multiple competing administrative systems
under different armed groups. Junta institutions functioning only in areas
under its military control.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2024 - Burma/Myanmar

2025.assessment:
Institutions remain collapsed or nominal under ongoing civil war. Junta
administering areas under its control; resistance groups administering
areas under theirs. No national institutions functioning.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2025

2005.assessment:
Low-level ethnic conflicts continued in border areas under SPDC military
junta rule. No mass-casualty events nationally. Sporadic fighting with
Karen, Shan, and other ethnic armed organizations. Political repression
through detention rather than mass violence.

2006.assessment:
Ongoing low-level ethnic conflict in Shan, Karen, Kachin, and other border
states. Military campaigns against ethnic armed organizations. Political
violence by state against civilians documented by HRW and Amnesty.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2007 - Myanmar

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution in September 2007 resulted in crackdown on protesting monks
and civilians. At least 31 people killed during protests per international observers,
with likely undercounting. Ongoing ethnic conflicts in border areas. State
repression intensified.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Crackdown: Repression of the 2007 Popular Protests in Burma, 2007

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis killed 130,000+ in May 2008, but these are not conflict deaths.
Ethnic conflict violence continued at low levels. The junta's obstruction of
international aid caused additional preventable deaths not captured as conflict deaths.

2009.assessment:
Military operations against Kokang ethnic group in Shan State in August 2009.
Ongoing ethnic conflicts in Karen, Kachin, and other areas. Low-level violence
continued throughout border regions.

2010.assessment:
Election year with managed transition. Low-level ethnic conflict continued.
Pressure on EAOs to transform into BGFs caused tensions. No major escalation
in violence nationally.

2011.assessment:
Kachin conflict resumed in June 2011 when the Kachin Independence Army (KIA)
ended its 17-year ceasefire. Significant fighting throughout northern Myanmar.
This was the year's major escalation. Multiple EAOs also engaged in conflict.

sources.citation: ICG Myanmar: A New Peace Initiative, 2011

2012.assessment:
Kachin conflict continued with significant military operations. Anti-Rohingya
violence in Rakhine State in June and October 2012 killed hundreds and displaced
100,000+. Multiple simultaneous conflict fronts.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, The Government Could Have Stopped This: Sectarian Violence and Ensuing Abuses, 2012

2013.assessment:
Continued ethnic conflict in Kachin, Shan, Karen. Anti-Muslim violence spread
to towns including Meiktila in March 2013 killing dozens. Overall violence
remained at moderate levels across multiple fronts.

2014.assessment:
Ethnic conflict continued. Peace process advancing with some groups.
Shan State conflict between Tatmadaw and MNDAA in March 2015 was significant.
Overall violence moderate but widespread.

2015.assessment:
Shan State conflict between MNDAA and Tatmadaw early in year. Nationwide
Ceasefire Agreement signed in October with 8 EAOs. Many major groups excluded.
Violence continued in Shan and Kachin states.

2016.assessment:
NLD government took power but violence continued. ARSA attacks on border posts
in northern Rakhine in October 2016 and military operations against Rohingya
followed. Shan and Kachin conflicts continued. Three Brotherhood Alliance formed.

2017.assessment:
Major escalation in 2017. ARSA attacks in August 2017 followed by catastrophic
military crackdown on Rohingya in northern Rakhine. Estimates of Rohingya killed
range from 10,000 to 25,000 (Médecins Sans Frontières estimated 10,000+ in Rakhine
alone). UN called it a textbook example of ethnic cleansing. 700,000+ fled to Bangladesh.
Kachin and Shan conflicts continued.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Shoot on Sight: Myanmar's Crackdown on the Rohingya in Rakhine, 2017
Médecins Sans Frontières, No refuge: Rohingya crisis, 2018

2018.assessment:
Continued violence in Rakhine (Arakan Army active), Kachin, and Shan states.
Military operations against AA in Rakhine. Shan State conflict ongoing. Violence
declined from 2017 Rohingya peak but remained elevated with multiple fronts.

2019.assessment:
Arakan Army (AA) conflict with Tatmadaw in Rakhine State intensified significantly
in 2019. Shan State conflicts continued with Northern Alliance. Kachin fighting
ongoing. Multiple fronts; elevated violence levels.

2020.assessment:
AA-Tatmadaw fighting in Rakhine major conflict. Ceasefire declared November 2020
following NLD election. Shan, Kachin conflicts continued. Pre-coup, violence at
moderate elevated level.

2021.assessment:
The February 2021 coup triggered nationwide violent resistance. Military killed
thousands of civilians and protesters. ACLED documented over 12,000 political
violence events. AAPP documented 1,500+ killed in protests. Civil war began with
PDF formation. Multiple EAOs broke ceasefires and joined resistance.
Catastrophic violence escalation.

2022.assessment:
Full-scale civil war. ACLED documented massive violence escalation. Military
air strikes on villages caused civilian casualties. Multiple PDF and EAO offensives.
Village burning documented. Death toll estimated at 12,000-25,000+ including
combatants and civilians. One of the world's most violent conflicts.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch World Report 2023 - Myanmar

2023.assessment:
Civil war continued with very high intensity. Operation 1027 from October 2023
opened new offensive by Brotherhood Alliance capturing major towns.
ACLED documented escalating violence. Air strikes on civilian areas continued.
Total deaths including all actors estimated at 20,000-35,000+ for the year.

2024.assessment:
Continuing high-intensity civil war. Resistance forces capturing more territory.
Junta's air force bombing campaigns causing civilian casualties. Major towns
including Lashio fell to resistance forces. Death toll remains very high
throughout the year.

2025.assessment:
Civil war continuing as of early 2026. Very high levels of violence persisting.
Junta losing more territory. Air strikes on civilian areas documented. No
resolution in sight.

2005.assessment:
The SPDC military junta controlled most of Myanmar's territory and all major
cities and infrastructure. However, ethnic armed organizations (EAOs) maintained
control of significant border areas — Karen, Kachin, Shan, and other ethnic states
had varying degrees of EAO presence. Estimated 10-20% of territory outside central
government effective control.

sources.citation: ICG Asia Report, Myanmar: Prospects for Change, 2005

2006.assessment:
Military junta maintained control of major population centers and economic
infrastructure. Ethnic armed organizations continued to control parts of
Shan, Karen, Kachin, and other border states with varying ceasefire arrangements.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution protests occurred in major cities but junta maintained
territorial control across population centers. Military crackdown reasserted
control after protests. EAOs continued to hold border areas.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis weakened state capacity in the Irrawaddy Delta but did not
change territorial control fundamentally. Junta maintained administrative
control. EAOs continued in border areas.

2009.assessment:
Junta maintained broad territorial control. Major military operations against
Kokang ethnic group in Shan State forced 37,000+ to flee to China. Tensions
with various EAOs continued.

2010.assessment:
Junta controlled major territory and population centers. Managed elections held.
EAOs that refused to transform into Border Guard Forces (BGFs) faced military
pressure. Kachin Independence Organization refused BGF, setting stage for
conflict in 2011.

2011.assessment:
Democratic transition government of Thein Sein took power. Kachin conflict
resumed after 17-year ceasefire when KIO refused to join BGFs. Fighting in
Kachin State from June 2011. Central government controlled major cities and
population centers but Kachin conflict added to contested territory.

2012.assessment:
Government signed preliminary ceasefires with several EAOs but Kachin conflict
continued. Anti-Muslim violence in Rakhine State created internal security
challenges. Territorial control broadly stable in most regions.

2013.assessment:
Peace process advancing with most EAOs. Central government control improved
with ceasefires. Kachin conflict continued. Shan State fighting continued.
Minor territorial changes but broadly status quo maintained.

2014.assessment:
Peace talks continued. Nationwide ceasefire framework negotiations active.
Fighting continued in Kachin and Shan states. Central government maintained
control of major population centers.

2015.assessment:
Nationwide Ceasefire Agreement (NCA) signed in October 2015 with 8 EAOs.
However major groups including KIA, UWSA, NDAA not included. Fighting continued
in Shan and Kachin states. Central government controlled core territory.

2016.assessment:
NLD government took power in April 2016. Peace process continued. ARSA attacks
in Rakhine in October 2016 led to military operations. Shan State fighting
continued. Central government broadly in control of major territory.

2017.assessment:
Rohingya crisis. Military crackdown in northern Rakhine from August 2017 forced
700,000+ Rohingya to flee to Bangladesh. Military controlled Rakhine territory
through devastating operations. Central government broadly controlled major
cities and lowlands. EAOs held border areas.

2018.assessment:
Central government broadly in control. Post-Rohingya crisis military maintained
operational control of Rakhine. Peace process continued with NCA signatories.
Fighting continued in Kachin and Shan states.

2019.assessment:
Central government controlled major population centers. Fighting in Rakhine
(ARSA and AA), Kachin, Shan continued. TNLA, MNDAA and AA formed Northern
Alliance intensifying pressure on military. Partial control contested in
northeast.

2020.assessment:
Central government controlled major population centers and lowlands.
Arakan Army (AA) fighting military in Rakhine State intensified in 2020.
Ceasefire declared in November 2020. Kachin, Shan conflict continued.
NLD re-elected November 2020.

2021.assessment:
Military coup February 1 reversed democratic government. Tatmadaw controlled
major cities initially but faced massive civil disobedience. People's Defence
Force (PDF) formed from CDM. Major new armed resistance emerged across country.
By end of 2021, military facing multiple fronts. Central control severely
challenged in many regions.

sources.citation: ICG Myanmar: Military Coup and Resistance, 2021

2022.assessment:
Civil war escalated. Junta controlled major cities and infrastructure, but
rural areas across many states contested. EAOs and PDF controlled large
swaths of Sagaing, Magway, Karen, Karenni, Chin, Kachin, and Shan states.
Estimated 40-50% of territory outside junta effective control by year end.

sources.citation: ICG Myanmar Coup: One Year Later, 2022

2023.assessment:
Junta losing territory throughout 2023. Operation 1027 launched by Brotherhood
Alliance (Three Brotherhood Alliance) in October 2023, capturing Chinshwehaw
and many towns in northern Shan State. Junta controlling less than majority of
territory by end of 2023. Multiple competing authorities across the country.

sources.citation: ICG Myanmar: A Year of Resistance, 2023

2024.assessment:
Junta continued losing territory in 2024. Multiple large towns captured by
resistance forces. Junta estimated to control less than 40% of territory by
mid-2024 according to various estimates. Multiple competing authorities including
NUG/CRPH-aligned forces, Brotherhood Alliance, KIO, AA, UWSA, and others.

2025.assessment:
As of early 2026, civil war continues with junta controlling significantly
less territory than before the coup. Multiple armed actors control different
parts of the country. Failed state conditions in many regions.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under SPDC military junta had virtually no budget transparency. No
public budget documents published. Military spending concealed. Multiple
exchange rates used to obscure fiscal reality. Citizens had no access to
any budget information.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006 (first year Myanmar assessed)

2006.assessment:
Complete budget opacity under SPDC junta. No public budget documents.
Military expenditure not disclosed. Multiple exchange rates obscured
true fiscal situation. OBI score minimal.

2007.assessment:
No budget transparency. SPDC junta did not publish budget documents.
Saffron Revolution year; economic hardship from fuel subsidy removal
not anticipated through any public fiscal planning.

2008.assessment:
Myanmar OBI score approximately 2/100 in IBP 2008 survey — among the
lowest globally. Virtually no public access to budget information.
Cyclone Nargis disaster relief not transparently managed.

2009.assessment:
Continued near-zero budget transparency. No public budget documents.
Military expenditure not visible. Junta's fiscal management completely opaque.

2010.assessment:
Near-zero budget transparency under SPDC junta. Election year but junta
budgets remained opaque. No public access to budget documents.

2011.assessment:
Marginal improvement with democratic transition beginning. Thein Sein
government began publishing some budget documents but transparency remained
very low. Military expenditure still not disclosed. OBI score near minimal.

2012.assessment:
Some improvement in budget transparency under Thein Sein reforms but
still very low. Budget documents began to be published in limited form.
Military spending still not disclosed. Some Western engagement pressured
transparency improvements.

2013.assessment:
