Budget transparency slowly improving under reform government. Myanmar
began participating in international budget transparency initiatives.
Still low by global standards but significant improvement from junta era.
Military spending remained opaque.

2014.assessment:
Continued slow improvement. Myanmar government publishing executive budget
proposal and some in-year reports. ASEAN chairmanship year may have pressured
some improvements. OBI remains very low globally.

2015.assessment:
Myanmar OBI score 18/100 in 2015 IBP survey — improved but still very
low. Some budget documents published publicly. NLD election created
expectations for improved transparency. Military spending still opaque.

2016.assessment:
NLD government took over but budget transparency remained low. Some budget
documents published. Military spending excluded. Myanmar still in bottom
tier globally for budget transparency.

2017.assessment:
OBI improved to 24/100 under NLD government. More budget documents publicly
available. Rohingya crisis spending not transparent. Military operations
budget opaque. Still in low-information category globally.

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained at modest level. NLD government continuing
to publish limited budget documents. Rohingya crisis context; humanitarian
spending opacity. OBI still low category.

2019.assessment:
OBI score approximately 30/100 in 2019 — further improvement under NLD.
More budget documents available but still below global median. Military
spending excluded. Legislature's oversight of budget improving marginally.

2020.assessment:
Pre-coup OBI maintained at modest level. NLD re-election. Budget transparency
continuing slow improvement trajectory. COVID-19 emergency spending not
transparently managed. Military spending still excluded from public accounts.

2021.assessment:
Coup in February 2021 destroyed budget transparency gains. SAC junta reverted
to junta-era opacity. No civilian budget oversight. IBP 2021 survey noted
collapse of transparency due to coup. Budget processes became completely opaque.

2022.assessment:
Near-complete budget opacity under SAC junta. Civil war conditions made
budget processes meaningless. Military spending dominant and completely
opaque. IBP unable to assess Myanmar properly under coup conditions.

2023.assessment:
Continuing budget opacity under SAC junta. Operation 1027 dramatically
expanded civil war. Military spending on operations completely opaque.
No meaningful budget transparency or public oversight process.

2024.assessment:
Complete budget opacity continuing. SAC junta losing territorial control
but maintaining military operations. No public budget process. Civil war
conditions preclude any meaningful budget transparency.

2025.assessment:
Budget opacity continuing. Civil war ongoing. No prospect of budget
transparency restoration under current conditions.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar not a member of EITI. Extractive sector (especially natural gas,
gems, jade, timber) operated under complete junta opacity. Jade and gem
exports estimated at billions but official figures unreliable. Gas revenues
from Yadana and Yetagun pipelines not publicly disclosed.

2006.assessment:
Complete opacity in extractive sector. Natural gas, jade, gems, timber all
extracted without any transparency. Revenue flows to military-controlled
entities (Myanmar Economic Corporation, MOGE). No EITI.

2007.assessment:
Complete extractive sector opacity. Gas exports to Thailand and China
generated significant revenues. Jade sales at Hpakant opaque. No EITI.
Saffron Revolution year — junta's fuel subsidy removal revealed gas
revenue management was completely non-transparent.

2008.assessment:
No EITI. Extractive sector completely opaque. Gas, jade, timber revenues
concealed. Cyclone Nargis raised questions about how gas revenues were
being used when disaster relief was inadequate.

2009.assessment:
Complete extractive opacity continuing. Shwe gas field development began.
Chinese involvement in Myitsone dam and related resource extraction opaque.
No EITI membership or application.

sources.citation: Earth Rights International Myanmar energy reports

2010.assessment:
No EITI. Extractive sector completely opaque. Gas revenues from multiple
pipelines to Thailand and China undisclosed. Jade mining at estimated
$3+ billion annually (Global Witness) but official figures far lower.

2011.assessment:
Transition year. Thein Sein government suspended Myitsone dam project
in September 2011, indicating some public accountability. EITI candidacy
not yet applied for but reform discussions beginning. Extractive sector
still largely opaque but signals of change.

2012.assessment:
Myanmar announced intention to join EITI in 2012 as part of broader
transparency reforms. Not yet a candidate. Extractive sector still largely
opaque but reform discussions ongoing. Some initial disclosures as part
of Western engagement.

2013.assessment:
Myanmar preparing for EITI candidacy application. Multi-stakeholder group
formation beginning. Extractive sector still largely opaque but reforms
underway. First EITI preparations creating some early transparency.
Global Witness documented jade revenues at $3.5 billion.

2014.assessment:
Myanmar admitted as EITI candidate in July 2014. This was a significant
transparency commitment — first extractive revenue disclosures beginning.
Myanmar EITI multi-stakeholder group established with civil society,
government, and company representatives. First EITI report commissioned.

2015.assessment:
EITI candidate. First EITI reconciliation reports published covering
oil, gas, and mining sectors. Discrepancies identified between government
and company revenue figures. Jade sector partially covered but significant
gaps remained. EITI process ongoing.

2016.assessment:
EITI candidate continuing. Annual EITI reconciliation reports covering
oil/gas/mining. NLD government supportive of EITI process. Jade sector
disclosures improving but Global Witness documented massive off-book
revenues ($31 billion jade value 2014-2015 mostly unreported).

sources.citation: EITI Myanmar reports 2016; Global Witness jade report

2017.assessment:
EITI candidate. Annual reports continuing. Myanmar working toward
EITI validation. Rohingya crisis created some political pressure but
EITI process continued. Extractive transparency improving in formal
sectors but informal/jade sector disclosures limited.

2018.assessment:
EITI candidate with ongoing validation process. Myanmar received
meaningful progress designation in EITI validation. Some gaps on
jade and gem sector disclosures noted. Military-owned extractive
companies (MEC) disclosures limited.

2019.assessment:
EITI candidate. Annual reports covering oil, gas, mining. Myanmar EITI
process continuing. Some improvement in disclosure scope. Jade and gem
mining transparency gaps persisting. Military-owned companies still
not fully disclosing under EITI framework.

2020.assessment:
EITI candidate. Pre-coup period. COVID-19 disrupted some EITI activities.
Myanmar EITI process continuing. Annual reconciliation reports published.
NLD re-election created expectation of continued EITI membership and
possible upgrade toward full compliance.

2021.assessment:
Coup in February 2021 immediately threatened Myanmar's EITI membership.
EITI Board suspended Myanmar in May 2021 due to junta's failure to meet
EITI requirements including multi-stakeholder group functionality (civil
society members could no longer participate safely). Myanmar EITI MSG
effectively dissolved. EITI suspended status from May 2021.

2022.assessment:
Myanmar remains suspended from EITI. No resumption of EITI reporting.
Extractive sector under junta completely opaque again. Chinese investment
in oil/gas continuing without EITI transparency. Jade revenues opaque.
Full regression to pre-EITI opacity.

2023.assessment:
Myanmar EITI suspension continuing. Process toward delisting underway.
Operation 1027 disrupted northern mining areas. Extractive revenues
completely opaque. Jade and gem mining in conflict zones with no oversight.
Civil war conditions make any extractive transparency impossible.

2024.assessment:
Myanmar formally delisted from EITI in February 2024 after failing to
meet conditions for reinstatement. Complete extractive opacity under
junta. Oil, gas, jade, gem revenues entirely opaque. Civil war preventing
any transparency mechanisms from operating.

2025.assessment:
Not a member of EITI (delisted 2024). Complete extractive opacity.
Civil war ongoing. No prospect of EITI rejoining under current conditions.

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under SPDC junta had no meaningful rule of law. Law functioned as
tool of oppression. No independent judiciary. Secret trials. Political
prisoners held without due process. Constitutional order suspended since
1988 coup. No legal transparency whatsoever. WJP did not assess Myanmar
in early editions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2005

2006.assessment:
Complete absence of rule of law. Judiciary not independent. Military
courts operating alongside civilian courts but no due process. Opposition
political parties and figures subject to arbitrary arrest. No legal
transparency for citizens.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2006

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution crackdown demonstrated complete absence of rule of
law. Monks, protesters, journalists arrested without due process. Special
courts set up to convict protesters. Laws used as weapons. No judicial
independence.

2008.assessment:
No rule of law. 2008 constitution drafted under junta but not yet
enacted. Judicial system completely subordinate to military. No
meaningful legal transparency. No independent bar association.

2009.assessment:
No rule of law. ASSK tried and convicted in mock trial (August 2009)
for allegedly violating house arrest (John Yettaw incident). Judiciary
completely subordinate to SPDC. No legal transparency.

2010.assessment:
No rule of law under junta. 2010 elections held without meaningful legal
framework for political competition. Judiciary not independent. ASSK
released from house arrest November 2010 but legal system still opaque.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010

2011.assessment:
Transition began with 2008 constitution enacted. Some legal reforms
initiated. Thein Sein government began releasing political prisoners.
Judiciary still not independent but some transition toward legal order
beginning. Very weak but transitional.

2012.assessment:
Legal system improving under reforms. Political prisoner releases. New
laws on labor, peaceful assembly. Bar association more active. Courts
still not fully independent but significant improvement from junta era.
WJP first included Myanmar in assessments.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Myanmar

2013.assessment:
Legal system reforms continuing. New investment law, company law, land
law reforms. Judiciary increasingly (marginally) independent. Still
significant systemic issues. Myanmar improving in WJP assessments but
still in very weak category.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Myanmar

2014.assessment:
Continuing legal reforms. Constitutional review process began but stalled
on military provisions. Courts still not fully independent. Land and
property law reforms. Rule of law improving but still weak by regional
standards.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index Myanmar

2015.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index score approximately 0.39/1.00 — low. Legal reforms
continuing but judiciary not fully independent. NLD election victory
created optimism for rule of law strengthening. Still significant gaps.
Among lower tier in East Asia/Pacific region.

2016.assessment:
NLD government brought modest legal improvements. New investment law,
company law reforms. Some judiciary reform. However, military retained
constitutional immunity from prosecution. Rakhine crisis reducing legal
protections for minorities.

2017.assessment:
Rule of law declining with Rohingya crisis. Security forces operating
outside any legal framework in Rakhine. Reuters journalists arrested
under Official Secrets Act. Military operations in Rakhine without
civilian oversight. WJP score declining slightly.

2018.assessment:
Rule of law declining post-Rohingya crisis. Journalists imprisoned under
Official Secrets Act. Military accountability for Rakhine crimes nonexistent.
Civilian courts functioning but with limitations. Military constitutionally
exempt from civilian legal oversight.

2019.assessment:
Rule of law stable but with significant limitations. Civilian courts
functioning for commercial disputes. Reuters journalists released.
ICJ proceedings against Myanmar for Rohingya genocide began. Military
constitutional immunity a major rule of law gap.

2020.assessment:
Pre-coup rule of law at modest level. Civilian courts functioning.
NLD re-elected with large majority. Military maintains constitutional
immunity. Legal system functional for commercial matters but political
and security matters still subject to military authority.

2021.assessment:
Coup completely destroyed rule of law. Civilian courts subordinated to
military. Special military courts established. ASSK and hundreds of
officials tried in mock courts. Lawyers for detainees arrested. Constitution
suspended. Decree law replaced legal order. Complete collapse.

2022.assessment:
No rule of law under SAC junta. Civil war conditions. Military decree law.
ASSK sentenced to multiple decades in prison in mock trials. Opposition
leaders executed. No independent judiciary. No legal transparency.

2023.assessment:
No rule of law continuing. Civil war with multiple competing legal
frameworks in different territories. Junta applying emergency decrees.
Resistance areas operating under PDF/NUG parallel legal systems.
No unified legal order across Myanmar's territory.

2024.assessment:
No functioning national rule of law. Junta controls some areas with
decree law. Resistance forces control other areas with their own legal
frameworks. ASSK remains imprisoned. No legal accountability for
military atrocities. WJP likely ranks Myanmar among worst globally.

2025.assessment:
Continuing absence of rule of law. Civil war ongoing. No prospect
of legal system restoration under current conditions.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index ongoing

2005.assessment:
Myanmar ranked among the world's worst press freedom environments. All
independent media banned. State media only. Journalists imprisoned.
Internet access severely restricted. No independent broadcasting.
One of the world's most censored countries alongside North Korea.

2006.assessment:
Near-complete state media control. No independent journalism permitted.
Foreign journalists banned. Internet heavily restricted. Bloggers arrested.
Myanmar consistently among bottom 5 globally for press freedom.

2007.assessment:
Saffron Revolution brought international media attention but junta cracked
down on coverage. Japanese journalist Kenji Nagai killed by security forces
while filming protests. Internet shut down during protests. RSF condemned
the killing. Complete state media control maintained.

2008.assessment:
Cyclone Nargis exposed junta's media control as international access to
disaster zone blocked. Bloggers and local journalists arrested for covering
cyclone aftermath. Internet cut. Complete state control over media.

2009.assessment:
Among worst globally for press freedom. Multiple journalists imprisoned
serving long sentences. All independent media outlets operated from exile
(Democratic Voice of Burma, Irrawaddy). Internet restricted.

2010.assessment:
Election year under complete media control. No critical election coverage
permitted. ASSK released from house arrest in November but media restrictions
unchanged. State broadcasters dominated all coverage. Near bottom globally.

2011.assessment:
Democratic transition began to relax some media restrictions. Thein Sein
government began easing censorship. Some political prisoners including
journalists released. Still severe restrictions but trajectory changing.
Internet access marginally more available.

2012.assessment:
Significant improvement in press freedom as Thein Sein government lifted
pre-publication censorship in August 2012. Private newspapers permitted.
Many journalists released from prison. Still significant restrictions but
major improvement from junta era. Internet expanding.

2013.assessment:
Further improvement with new media laws allowing private newspapers and
broadcast media. Daily newspapers launched. Significant press freedom gains.
Still restrictions on coverage of military and ethnic conflicts. Self-censorship
remained common. Continued improvement trajectory.

2014.assessment:
Press freedom improving but limitations remaining. Journalists still
prosecuted for coverage of military or Rakhine violence. Telecom expansion
improved information access. Self-censorship on sensitive topics. ASEAN
chairmanship year; some media openness maintained.

2015.assessment:
Election year saw vibrant media coverage of NLD campaign. Press freedom
improved significantly. Private TV, newspapers, and digital media operating.
Restrictions on military coverage remained. RSF rank improved to middle tier.
Internet penetration growing rapidly with mobile phone expansion.

2016.assessment:
NLD government took over. Press freedom broadly maintained. Private media
continued to expand. Internet access widened. Restrictions on Rakhine
coverage emerging with ARSA attacks in October 2016. RSF rank stable.

2017.assessment:
Rohingya crisis severely restricted press freedom in Rakhine State. Reuters
journalists Daniel Fenwick and Kyaw Soe Oo arrested December 2017 for
reporting on Rohingya massacre (Inn Din massacre). Internet shutdown in
Rakhine. Media self-censorship on Rohingya extensively documented. RSF
cited these restrictions despite overall Myanmar rank improvement.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017; Reuters journalist arrests

2018.assessment:
Deteriorating press freedom due to Rohingya crisis aftermath. Reuters
journalists Wa Lone and Kyaw Soe Oo convicted under Official Secrets Act
and imprisoned for 7 years (September 2018). Internet shutdown continued
in parts of Rakhine and Kachin. Self-censorship widespread. RSF rank
dropped significantly.

2019.assessment:
Reuters journalists released May 2019 following presidential pardon.
Internet shutdowns in Rakhine and Chin continuing. Ongoing use of
defamation laws against journalists. Press freedom severely constrained
in conflict areas while relatively functional in major cities.

2020.assessment:
Internet shutdown in parts of Rakhine and Chin continued (longest in
Myanmar's history — over a year). Defamation laws used against journalists.
Election year media coverage largely functional in Yangon/Naypyidaw.
Pre-coup baseline of significant restrictions.

2021.assessment:
Coup in February 2021 catastrophically destroyed press freedom. All private
media outlets raided and shut down. Journalists mass-arrested. Military
revoked licenses of leading independent media (Mizzima, DVB, Myanmar Now,
Eleven Media). Internet shut down repeatedly. Journalists killed covering
protests. RSF documented dramatic collapse.

2022.assessment:
Myanmar ranked 176/180 globally — among the absolute worst. Journalists
imprisoned, killed, or driven into exile. Independent media operating
clandestinely from border areas or abroad. Internet restricted. Civil
war making journalism extremely dangerous.

2023.assessment:
Continuing near-complete suppression of press freedom. Myanmar among bottom
5 globally. Dozens of journalists in prison. Independent reporting only
from exile outlets (Irrawaddy, DVB, Myanmar Now operating from border/exile).
Operation 1027 areas inaccessible to journalists.

2024.assessment:
Continuing near-bottom press freedom. Journalists in prison or exile.
Civil war makes independent reporting dangerous. Junta territory has no
free press. In resistance-held territories, some independent information
flow but limited formal media. RSF continues to rank Myanmar among worst.

2025.assessment:
Continuing complete suppression of press freedom. Civil war ongoing.
No independent media operating openly inside Myanmar.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index ongoing

2005.assessment:
Myanmar under SPDC junta had extremely poor statistical capacity. Central
Statistical Organization (CSO) published limited data. Multiple exchange
rates made national accounts unreliable. No population census since 1983.
Economic data unreliable due to junta manipulation. Statistical capacity
among lowest in Asia.

sources.citation: World Bank Myanmar statistical capacity assessments

2006.assessment:
Very low statistical capacity. CSO data unreliable. National accounts
not internationally comparable. No independent verification of official
statistics. GDP estimates widely divergent between official and IMF/WB.

2008.assessment:
Very low statistical capacity. Cyclone Nargis disaster — humanitarian
statistics for affected population unreliable. National statistics
system dysfunctional by international standards.

2009.assessment:
Continued very low statistical capacity. IMF Article IV noted inability
to rely on official economic statistics. No internationally comparable
GDP or trade data available.

2010.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained very low under junta's final year. Election
statistics of questionable reliability. No census. Multiple exchange rates
continued to distort national accounts.

2011.assessment:
Statistical capacity score approximately 36/100 — extremely low. Transition
government began reform of statistical systems. Single exchange rate
adoption in 2012 would improve national accounts. Reform trajectory beginning.

2012.assessment:
Statistical capacity improving with exchange rate unification (April 2012).
National accounts becoming more reliable. CSO reforms underway with
international technical assistance. First census preparations began.
Significant improvement from junta baseline but still very weak.

2013.assessment:
Statistical capacity improving significantly. International technical
assistance for CSO reform from World Bank, ADB, DFID. Census preparations
ongoing (population census conducted March 2014). Data quality improving
across economic, demographic, and social statistics.

2014.assessment:
Population and Housing Census conducted March 2014 — first since 1983.
Major improvement in demographic and socioeconomic data. Statistical
capacity improving significantly. SCI reaching moderate levels. International
assistance driving improvements.

2015.assessment:
Statistical capacity at moderate level. Census data being processed and
published. National accounts using 2015-16 base year. International
technical assistance continuing. NLD election created optimism for
further data modernization.

2016.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable at moderate level. NLD government continued
CSO reform programs. International statistics partnerships maintained.
Myanmar Statistical Information Service (MMSIS) portal launched for
data access. Moderate but improving.

2017.assessment:
Statistical capacity improving but Rohingya crisis distorted some data.
Population figures for Rakhine State uncertain. Economic statistics
improving. SCI continuing positive trajectory. Myanmar improving
data quality with international support.

2018.assessment:
Statistical capacity at moderate level. Post-Rohingya crisis; some
demographic data gaps in Rakhine. Economic statistics improving.
National accounts, balance of payments, and trade statistics in good
shape for Southeast Asian LDC. International assistance continued.

2019.assessment:
Statistical capacity approaching moderate-good level for LDC. Myanmar CSO
increasingly publishing data on MMSIS portal. International comparability
improving. SCI at 65/100 approaching peer LDC level.

2020.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained. COVID-19 disrupted some data collection
but core CSO functions maintained. Pre-coup statistical system had become
reasonably functional for LDC. WB SCI at approximately 65/100. NLD
election statistics tracked.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (final year published)

2021.assessment:
Coup in February 2021 destroyed statistical system improvements. CSO
staff participated in Civil Disobedience Movement. Data collection
collapsed. International partnerships suspended. Statistical capacity
returning to near-junta-era levels. WB SCI series discontinued in 2020
so no comparable post-coup data available.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Performance Indicators (new series)

2022.assessment:
Statistical capacity severely degraded under civil war. Junta publishes
some economic data but reliability extremely low. Population displaced
statistics incomplete. Civil war areas have no data collection.
International organizations unable to verify statistics.

2023.assessment:
Continuing collapse of statistical capacity. Operation 1027 displacement
and fighting throughout northern Myanmar makes any national data collection
impossible. Junta economic data of very low reliability. International
statistical assistance suspended.

2024.assessment:
No functioning national statistical system. Civil war conditions make
data collection impossible across large portions of country. Any junta
statistics are unreliable. No internationally comparable data.

2025.assessment:
Continuing collapse of statistical capacity. Civil war ongoing.
No prospect of statistical system restoration under current conditions.
